content:
# Mark Chapter 11 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Mark Chapter 11 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 11:2 — "colt" vs. "young donkey" for πῶλον

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a-colt tied" | πῶλος = young animal, typically a donkey. |
| LOGOS | "a colt tied" | Same. |
| PNEUMA | "a young donkey tied up" | Specifies "donkey" for clarity. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "colt" — πῶλος is the Greek term as written, and "colt" preserves the traditional rendering. Mark (unlike Matthew) does not explicitly mention a donkey, and the Zechariah 9:9 allusion operates through the detail of an unridden animal.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 11:8 — "garments" vs. "cloaks" for ἱμάτια

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "their garments" | Literal; ἱμάτιον = outer garment. |
| LOGOS | "their cloaks" | "Cloaks" for the outer garment. |
| PNEUMA | "their cloaks" | Same as LOGOS. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "cloaks" — ἱμάτιον refers specifically to the outer cloak/mantle, and "cloaks" is both accurate and vivid.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 11:10 — "our father David" vs. "our ancestor David" for τοῦ πατρὸς ἡμῶν Δαυίδ

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "our father David" | Literal; πατήρ = father. |
| LOGOS | "our father David" | Same. |
| PNEUMA | "our ancestor David" | "Ancestor" to avoid confusion with immediate father. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "our father David" — πατήρ in this patriarchal/genealogical sense means "forefather," and "father" is the traditional, biblically resonant rendering (cf. "Abraham our father").

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 11:15 — "those who sold and those who bought" vs. "the merchants and customers"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to-cast-out those selling and those buying in the temple" | Literal; participial construction. |
| LOGOS | "to drive out those who sold and those who bought in the temple" | Formal; parallel structure preserved. |
| PNEUMA | "driving out the merchants and customers" | Dynamic; simplifies to nouns. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "to drive out those who sold and those who bought in the temple" — the participial structure (τοὺς πωλοῦντας καὶ τοὺς ἀγοράζοντας) emphasizes the ongoing commercial activity, and the parallel construction is important.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 11:16 — "carry a vessel" vs. "carry anything" vs. "use the temple courts as a shortcut"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "carry a-vessel through the temple" | Literal; σκεῦος = vessel/implement. |
| LOGOS | "carry anything through the temple" | Generalizes σκεῦος. |
| PNEUMA | "use the temple courts as a shortcut for carrying goods" | Interprets the historical practice. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "carry anything through the temple" — σκεῦος can mean vessel, implement, or goods. LOGOS's "anything" captures the comprehensive prohibition. PNEUMA's "shortcut" is historically accurate but adds interpretation.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 11:17 — "den of robbers" vs. "hideout for thieves" for σπήλαιον λῃστῶν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a-den of-robbers" | Literal; Jeremiah 7:11 quotation. |
| LOGOS | "a den of robbers" | Same. |
| PNEUMA | "a hideout for thieves" | Dynamic; "hideout" and "thieves" for modern clarity. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "a den of robbers" — this is a direct quotation from Jeremiah 7:11, and the traditional English rendering should be preserved for intertextual recognition.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 11:22 — "faith of God" vs. "faith in God" for πίστιν θεοῦ

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Have faith of-God." | Preserves the genitive ambiguity. |
| LOGOS | "Have faith in God." | Objective genitive interpretation. |
| PNEUMA | "Have faith in God." | Same as LOGOS. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Have faith in God" — the objective genitive is the natural reading in this imperative context. While the subjective genitive ("God's faithfulness") is theologically rich, it does not fit the exhortatory context as naturally.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 11:23 — "Be taken up and thrown" vs. "Pick yourself up and throw yourself"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "'Be-taken-up and be-thrown into the sea'" | Passive imperatives. |
| LOGOS | "'Be taken up and thrown into the sea'" | Same. |
| PNEUMA | "'Pick yourself up and throw yourself into the sea'" | Active/reflexive; personifies the mountain. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted the passive "Be taken up and thrown into the sea" — the Greek uses passive imperatives (ἄρθητι καὶ βλήθητι), and the passive voice implies divine agency, not the mountain acting on itself.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 11:25 — "trespasses" vs. "sins" for παραπτώματα

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "your trespasses" | Traditional; παράπτωμα = trespass/transgression. |
| LOGOS | "your trespasses" | Same. |
| PNEUMA | "your sins" | Simplified; general term. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "trespasses" — παράπτωμα specifically means "a falling aside" (trespass/transgression), distinct from ἁμαρτία (sin/missing the mark). The distinction is worth preserving.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 11:30 — "from heaven or from man" vs. "from God, or was it merely human"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "from heaven or from men" | Literal; ἐξ οὐρανοῦ ἢ ἐξ ἀνθρώπων. |
| LOGOS | "from heaven or from man" | Same; singular "man." |
| PNEUMA | "from God, or was it merely human" | Interprets "heaven" as "God"; adds "merely." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "from heaven or from man" — "heaven" is a Jewish circumlocution for God, and preserving it maintains the cultural texture. PNEUMA's "from God" is accurate but flattens the idiom.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Occurrences | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| **OT quotation preservation** (den of robbers, Psalm 118) | vv. 10, 17 | Traditional wording retained for intertextual recognition |
| **Jewish idiom preservation** ("heaven" for God, "father David") | vv. 10, 30 | Idioms preserved with their cultural texture |
| **Genitive ambiguity** (πίστιν θεοῦ) | v. 22 | Resolved by context |
| **Passive vs. active voice** | v. 23 | Greek voice preserved for theological implication |
| **PNEUMA's interpretive additions** (shortcut, hideout, ancestor) | vv. 10, 16, 17 | Generally not adopted |

**Overall Pattern Analysis:** Mark 11 is dominated by the temple action and its framing fig tree story. The translators show strong agreement on the narrative and on preserving the OT quotations (Isaiah 56:7, Jeremiah 7:11, Psalm 118:22-23). The most significant disagreement involves the genitive in v. 22 (πίστιν θεοῦ), which EMET preserves as ambiguous while LOGOS and PNEUMA resolve as objective genitive. The consensus follows the majority. The chapter demonstrates that PNEUMA's historically informed paraphrases (temple shortcut, v. 16) are valuable as commentary but generally not adopted into the translation text.
